content.text: SLOs and SLAs Are Not the Same

A Service Level Objective (SLO) is an internal engineering commitment: the target your team sets for a metric and works to maintain. A Service Level Agreement (SLA) is an external contractual commitment: the guarantee you make to customers, often with financial penalties (service credits) if breached. Conflating them is one of the most common mistakes in reliability engineering.

The SLO must be set tighter than the SLA. If you commit to customers that you will deliver 99.7% availability and your internal SLO is also 99.7%, you have no buffer — any SLO breach is simultaneously an SLA breach with contractual consequences. The gap between the SLO and the SLA is the reliability margin, and it exists for operational reasons, not as padding.
SLA = the floor below which customers have a contractual remedy. SLO = the target your team works to. The gap between them is the reliability margin — it must be non-zero.

Every dimension of the customer SLA should map to an internal SLO. The SLA commitment is derived from the SLO by subtracting the reliability margin. The reliability margin accounts for: measurement methodology differences between how you measure internally vs how customers verify externally; upstream variability from Anthropic's own API (which you do not control); and the need to take action before the SLA is breached.

attrs.alt: SLO-to-SLA mapping cascade showing internal SLOs, reliability margin, and customer-committed SLA values

content.text: The reliability margin is not arbitrary. It should be large enough that a breach of the SLO gives you time to detect and mitigate before the SLA is breached. For a 99.9% SLO and a 99.7% SLA, the margin is 0.2 percentage points — which translates to about 86 minutes of additional allowed downtime per 30 days. This gives the team time to respond to an SLO breach before it becomes an SLA breach.
What to Include in a Customer SLA

Customer SLAs for Claude API integration services typically cover availability, optionally latency, and incident response times. They do not include quality metrics (finish_reason, content filter rates) — these are too variable and depend on caller-controlled inputs (prompt content, max_tokens settings).
The availability commitment is the most common SLA term. It states the minimum percentage of requests that will succeed (return a valid response) in a given calendar month.

## Availability Commitment

Anthropic [or: Your Company] will maintain API availability of 99.7% in any
calendar month, measured as the percentage of API requests that return a
non-5xx HTTP response.

**Excluded from availability calculation:**
- Requests that fail due to customer-supplied invalid parameters (4xx responses).
- Requests during scheduled maintenance windows (notified ≥72 hours in advance).
- Failures caused by customer infrastructure, client-side network issues, or
  third-party dependencies outside Anthropic's control.
- Rate-limited requests (HTTP 429) where the customer has exceeded their
  contracted rate limit.

**Measurement:**
Availability is calculated from Anthropic's internal telemetry. In the event
of a dispute, Anthropic will provide supporting data within 10 business days.

Latency commitments are less common in SLAs because they are highly dependent on the model selected, the length of the prompt and response, and the customer's network path. When included, they are typically expressed as a percentile threshold for a specific model tier, not as an across-the-board guarantee.

## Response Time Commitment (Optional — Model-Specific)

For requests to the claude-3-haiku-20240307 model with max_tokens ≤ 1024,
Anthropic targets a p95 response time of 2.0 seconds or below, measured at
the API gateway.

This commitment applies to non-streaming requests only. Streaming requests
have no response-time commitment; instead, a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of
800 ms p95 is targeted as a best-effort metric and does not carry credit terms.

Anthropic does not make latency commitments for the claude-3-opus-20240229
or claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620 models due to the variable nature of model
inference time.

Incident response commitments define how quickly Anthropic will acknowledge, communicate about, and resolve production incidents. They are separate from availability commitments — a service can be 'available' during a degraded incident.
Once on detection, once on resolution

Service credits are the financial remedy customers receive when an SLA is breached. They are typically expressed as a percentage of the monthly bill applied as account credit (not cash). The credit tiers are structured to provide meaningful compensation without creating perverse incentives.

Customers must submit a credit claim within 30 days of the incident. The claim must include the incident date, the affected time window, and a description of the impact. Anthropic reviews claims against internal telemetry and issues credits within 30 days of claim submission.
Credits are applied to the next billing cycle, not issued as cash.
Total credits in any calendar month are capped at 50% of the monthly bill.
Credits do not apply to incidents caused by customer error, force majeure, or third-party failures.
Customers forfeit credit eligibility if they fail to notify Anthropic of the breach within the claim window.
Service credits are not the primary reliability incentive — the internal error budget policy is. Credits are a contractual safety net, not the primary mechanism for driving reliability work.
A well-written SLA is as much about what is excluded as what is guaranteed. Exclusions prevent the SLA from becoming a catch-all for every outage regardless of cause.
Force majeure: natural disasters, power grid failures, regulatory actions outside Anthropic's control.
Scheduled maintenance: notified in advance via status page and email. Maintenance windows do not count against availability.
Customer-caused failures: invalid API usage, exceeding rate limits, deploying misconfigured integrations.
Third-party infrastructure: Anthropic's cloud infrastructure provider (AWS, GCP) outages that affect the full region.
Upstream model provider outages: if the model inference layer is operated by a third party, outages there are excluded.
Beta features: SLA commitments do not apply to features explicitly labeled 'beta' or 'preview'.
Many customers interpret 'availability' as 'the service does what I expect in all cases'. SLA availability commitments cover only whether the service returned a non-5xx response. They do not cover:
Response quality: whether the model output was useful, relevant, or accurate.
Content filter triggering: responses that are blocked by safety systems are HTTP 200 with stop_reason='content_filter'.
Token budget: responses truncated by max_tokens are HTTP 200 with stop_reason='max_tokens'.
Rate limit 429s where the customer exceeded their contracted limit.
Model behavior changes: model output distributions can shift between versions — this is not an SLA event.
Aligning SLA Measurement with Customer Measurement

A common source of SLA disputes is measurement methodology disagreement. Anthropic measures availability at its API gateway; the customer measures it at their client. These two measurements can differ significantly due to network variability between them.
Server-side success rate excluding client network issues
Primary: used for SLA calculations and credit determinations
Secondary: used to flag disputes; not authoritative for credits
Publicly declared incidents and their duration
Reference: incident timeline used in credit claim review

When customer-observed availability differs from Anthropic-measured availability, the standard dispute process requires both parties to share their measurement data (request logs with timestamps, HTTP response codes, and request IDs) within 10 business days of the claim. Discrepancies between the two can usually be explained by:
Network path latency causing client-side timeouts for requests that succeeded server-side.
DNS resolution failures between customer infrastructure and Anthropic API endpoints.
CDN or proxy layers introducing failures not visible to Anthropic's instrumentation.
Client-side retry logic counting a single server success as a failure (if the retry was the one that succeeded).

Customer-facing SLA communication has two channels: real-time status (the status page) and post-incident reports (incident post-mortems shared with enterprise customers). Both channels must be operated consistently with the SLA commitments.
Status page must update within 15 minutes of a P0/Critical incident declaration.
Updates must include: incident description, affected services, current status (investigating/identified/monitoring/resolved), and next update time.
Status page history must be accurate and complete — a missing incident undermines trust more than the incident itself.
Scheduled maintenance must appear on the status page at least 72 hours before it begins.

Enterprise customers on custom SLA terms typically expect a post-incident report (PIR) within 5 business days of any P0/Critical incident. The PIR is not a public post-mortem — it is a customer-facing document that explains impact, root cause, remediation, and steps taken to prevent recurrence.

## Post-Incident Report Template (Customer-Facing)

**Incident date:** 2024-04-15
**Affected service:** Claude API v1/messages endpoint
**Duration:** 38 minutes (14:32 – 15:10 UTC)
**Impact:** Approximately 2% of requests experienced elevated latency
(p99 response time 3.2× above baseline). No requests failed; all returned
valid responses.

**Root cause:**
A configuration change to the model serving layer introduced a memory
allocation regression in the tokenizer. Under load, this caused the model
server's garbage collector to pause for 50–150ms per request, increasing
p99 latency from 800ms to 2.6s.

**Timeline:**
- 14:32 UTC: Alert fired on p99 latency exceeding SLO threshold
- 14:37 UTC: On-call engineer acknowledged; investigation began
- 14:52 UTC: Root cause identified; rollback initiated
- 15:10 UTC: Rollback complete; latency returned to baseline
- 15:25 UTC: All-clear declared; incident closed

**Remediation:**
- Tokenizer configuration change rolled back to prior version.
- Memory profiling added to pre-production test suite to catch this class
  of regression before deployment.
- Canary rollout extended from 1% to 5% before full rollout for this
  service tier.

**SLA status:**
This incident does not trigger SLA credit eligibility. Availability remained
at 100% throughout; only latency was affected. Latency SLA thresholds
(90% of requests under 3s) were not breached.

Enterprise API services often offer multiple SLA tiers at different price points. Lower-tier customers get a base availability commitment; enterprise customers get tighter commitments, faster incident response SLAs, and dedicated post-incident reports.

When designing tiers, ensure your internal SLO comfortably exceeds the tightest SLA tier. If you offer 99.9% to enterprise customers, your internal SLO should target 99.95% or better — otherwise every incident that hits enterprise customers triggers a credit liability.
SLAs must be reviewed periodically to ensure they still reflect the service's capabilities and the team's commitments. A signed SLA that is never reviewed becomes a liability as the service changes.
Architecture changes: adding a new region, changing the model serving infrastructure, migrating to a new API gateway.
Reliability track record: if the service has been meeting 99.95% availability for 12 consecutive months, the SLA may be tightened.
Tier pricing changes: if a new enterprise tier is added, its SLA must be scoped before the tier launches.
Post-incident: if an incident causes an SLA breach, review whether the SLA's exclusions were applied correctly and whether the credit calculation was accurate.
Internal SLO vs SLA Alignment Check

# sla_alignment_check.py — verify internal SLOs exceed all SLA commitments
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SLACommitment:
    tier: str
    availability_pct: float
    latency_sla: str | None  # e.g. "90% under 3s" or None

@dataclass
class InternalSLO:
    name: str
    availability_target: float
    latency_target: str | None

def check_alignment(slos: list[InternalSLO], slas: list[SLACommitment]) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of alignment issues; empty list means all is well."""
    issues = []
    tightest_availability_sla = max(slas, key=lambda s: s.availability_pct)
    primary_slo = next((s for s in slos if s.name == "claude-integration-availability"), None)

    if primary_slo is None:
        issues.append("No primary availability SLO found matching 'claude-integration-availability'")
        return issues

    margin = primary_slo.availability_target - tightest_availability_sla.availability_pct
    if margin < 0.1:
        issues.append(
            f"Reliability margin too thin: SLO {primary_slo.availability_target}% "
            f"vs tightest SLA {tightest_availability_sla.availability_pct}% "
            f"({margin:.3f}% margin — minimum recommended: 0.1%)"
        )
    return issues

# Example usage
slas = [
    SLACommitment("standard", 99.5, None),
    SLACommitment("business", 99.7, "90% under 3s"),
    SLACommitment("enterprise", 99.9, "90% under 3s"),
]
slos = [
    InternalSLO("claude-integration-availability", 99.95, None),
    InternalSLO("claude-integration-latency", 99.0, "95% under 2s"),
]

issues = check_alignment(slos, slas)
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"ALIGNMENT ISSUE: {issue}")
else:
    print("SLO/SLA alignment: OK")
# → SLO/SLA alignment: OK (99.95% SLO vs 99.9% tightest SLA = 0.05% margin — barely OK)

Customer-facing SLAs are derived from internal SLOs by subtracting a reliability margin — the margin ensures that an SLO breach triggers internal action before the SLA is breached and contractual remedies apply. SLAs cover availability (fraction of requests returning non-5xx) and optionally response-time commitments for specific model tiers; they do not cover response quality, content filter triggers, or model behavior. Service credits are the contractual remedy for SLA breaches; they are capped at a fraction of the monthly bill and do not apply to excluded categories. Measurement methodology must be agreed upon in advance to prevent disputes — Anthropic's API gateway is the authoritative measurement point. Multi-tier SLAs allow differentiation by price tier; the internal SLO must always exceed the tightest SLA tier by a meaningful margin.
SLA = SLO - reliability margin. Never set the SLA at the same level as the SLO.
Reliability margin absorbs: measurement differences, upstream variability, and response time before intervention.
SLA availability covers: non-5xx responses. Does NOT cover quality, content filters, or rate-limited 429s.
Service credits: tiered by severity, capped at 50% of monthly bill, applied as account credit not cash.
Measurement point: Anthropic API gateway is authoritative; customer-side measurements are for dispute evidence only.
Multi-tier SLAs: enterprise commitments must be tighter than the SLO — verify alignment before signing contracts.
